coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electrochemical efficiency of the open porous sandrose structured electrocatalyst for sustainable hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pitchai, Chandrasekaran
Vedanarayanan, Mahalakshmi
Chen, Chih-Ming
Gopalakrishnan, Sethuraman Mathur
coredata.dc:description: Employing highly efficient and durable electrocatalysts shows considerable potential in tackling the energy crisis, especially in applications concerning the production of both H2 and O2 via water splitting. In this research, we adopted a simple hydrothermal method to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NDCDs) from fruit waste. Subsequently, we synthesized a composite material comprising CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs by integrating a cobalt-iron layered double hydroxide (CoFe-LDH) and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) with the NDCDs. The resulting CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs featured a highly porous sandrose structure that facilitates electron flow pathways. Additionally, this nanocomposite had a larger and more open surface area, reducing resistance. The streamlined electron pathways and improved electrolyte diffusion contributed to outstanding catalytic performance in both the HER and OER; a current density of 10 mA cm⁻2 was achieved with minimal overpotentials. The intrinsic activities, including the TOF and specific activity, of the composite demonstrated its remarkable electrocatalytic performance for the HER and OER. Furthermore, the synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite demonstrated enhanced stability for 24 h, as confirmed by chronopotentiometry. This study validates the effectiveness of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite, positioning it as a compelling electrocatalyst for water splitting reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bifunctional electrocatalyst
Fruit waste
CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs
Sandrose
Higher stability

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Chandrasekaran Pitchai and Chih-Ming Chen thank the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan for funding fellowship (NSTC 112-2811-E\u2212005-002 and NSTC 110-2221-E\u2212005-006-MY3). Chih-Ming Chen thank the financial support from the \u201CInnovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture\u201D from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan.
Chandrasekaran Pitchai and Chih-Ming Chen thank the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan for funding fellowship (NSTC 112-2811-E-005-002 and NSTC 110-2221-E-005-006-MY3).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs, CoFe-LDH, and MoS2.
a&b) FE-SEM and c-e) FE-TEM images of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs with different magnifications.
XPS for CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs a) deconvolution spectra of Co 2p, b) Fe 2p c) O 1s d) Mo 3d e) S 2p and f) N 1s levels.
The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of CoFe-LDH, CoFe-LDH@MoS2, and CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs
a) Linear sweep voltammetry and b) Tafel slopes for synthesized catalysts.
a) Linear sweep voltammetry and b) Tafel slopes for synthesized catalysts.
Nyquist plots for CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF, CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF and bare NF.
a) CV curves for CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF at different scan rates, b-d) linear fit of current density vs scan rates for CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF, CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF, CoFe-LDH/NF.
Area of the reduction peaks for a) CoFe-LDH, b) CoFe-LDH@MoS2, and c) CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs
TOF values of synthesized catalyst for a) HER and b) OER at 300 mV.
Chronopotentiometry curve of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF at 10 mA/cm2 for 24 h of a) HER and b) OER.
Summary of the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the LDH materials.

head.ce:title: Enhanced electrochemical efficiency of the open porous sandrose structured electrocatalyst for sustainable hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, Taichung, 402202, Taiwan.
Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)Gandhigram, Dindigul, Tamil Nadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Employing highly efficient and durable electrocatalysts shows considerable potential in tackling the energy crisis, especially in applications concerning the production of both H2 and O2 via water splitting. In this research, we adopted a simple hydrothermal method to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NDCDs) from fruit waste. Subsequently, we synthesized a composite material comprising CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs by integrating a cobalt-iron layered double hydroxide (CoFe-LDH) and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) with the NDCDs. The resulting CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs featured a highly porous sandrose structure that facilitates electron flow pathways. Additionally, this nanocomposite had a larger and more open surface area, reducing resistance. The streamlined electron pathways and improved electrolyte diffusion contributed to outstanding catalytic performance in both the HER and OER; a current density of 10 mA cm⁻2 was achieved with minimal overpotentials. The intrinsic activities, including the TOF and specific activity, of the composite demonstrated its remarkable electrocatalytic performance for the HER and OER. Furthermore, the synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite demonstrated enhanced stability for 24 h, as confirmed by chronopotentiometry. This study validates the effectiveness of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite, positioning it as a compelling electrocatalyst for water splitting reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of the world's population and increasing environmental pollution have amplified the demand for energy. This has led to a focused investigation into renewable electricity sources. The quest for alternative energy solutions is fueled by the pressing goal of alleviating the escalating effects of climate emergencies [1]. The significance of replacing fossil fuels through the use of cost-effective catalysts to meet energy requirements is highly regarded. The energy supplied through the electrochemical water splitting reaction occurs via the HER and OER [2,3]. One hurdle to the anodic process of oxygen evolution is the higher potential required due to the formation of O–O bonds in the 4e-transfer process [4]. Conversely, the cathodic process of hydrogen evolution necessitates a lower potential, rendering it more advantageous than the anodic region [5]. Thus, a crucial focus for this reaction is the creation of a catalyst that enhances equally the anodic and cathodic reactions. Several researchers have passionately dedicated their work to producing electrocatalysts based on non-noble metals. This endeavor aims to replace expensive noble metals such as Pt and Ru for the HER and OER, respectively [6,7]. Building on this foundation, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a type of 2D metal dichalcogenide, possesses sulfur-terminated edges that hold the potential for highly active catalytic behavior [8]. Although MoS2 has garnered recognition for its effectiveness in the HER, achieving superior OER activity necessitates its combination with metal hydroxides, such as layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [9,10]. Owing to the hydroxyl-containing surface host layers, LDHs function as exceptional catalysts for improving the kinetics of the OER. Furthermore, the integration of LDHs with MoS2 enhances the adsorption of hydrogen on the MoS2 surface, leading to a significant acceleration of the electrochemical activity at an even faster rate [11,12]. Furthermore, studies have indicated that various carbon allotropes, such as carbon nanowires, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots, serve effectively as electron-conducting materials. They facilitate rapid electron transport and simultaneously increase the surface area, thereby significantly stabilizing the catalyst structure [13].

Feng et al. detailed the synthesis of CoFe-LDH and employed it as an electrocatalyst for the OER, achieving an overpotential of 300 mV at 10 mA/cm2 [14]. Recognizing its comparatively lower activity, Bhowmik et al. explored the formation and application of composites involving carbon-related materials, such as graphitic carbon nitrides combined with LDHs, for water oxidation purposes [15]. Guo et al. fabricated CoFe LDH with rGO-modified Ni foam and employed it as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for both HER and OER [16]. Yao et al. investigated the synthesis and application for overall water splitting of composites containing NiTe@CoFe LDH [17]. Tong et al. synthesized vanadium doped nickel sulphide supported CoFe LDH and utilized as OER catalyst effectively [18]. Alotaibi et al. developed molybdenum oxide nanoparticles [19], tungsten-based oxides [20] and manganese-based oxides [21] as electrocatalyst for both HER and OER. Li et al. observed that incorporating CoFe-LDH with N-doped porous carbon nanosheets enhanced the electrical conductivity but did not improve the intrinsic OER performance [22]. To address this limitation, we developed NDCD-supported CoFe-LDH/MoS2 as a replacement electrocatalyst for the OER and HER. The nitrogen in the NDCDs contributed to a higher density of exposed active sites, enhancing the catalytic activity by converting carbon atoms into active sites. Specifically, the nitrogen-containing groups facilitated combination with protons, promoting the formation of carbonaceous composites with numerous active sites. NDCDs not only significantly improved the catalytic properties but also inhibited the corrosion of the catalyst, ensuring good stability [23–25]. In addition, carbon dots (CDs) are affordable and environmentally friendly nanoparticles. They offer several advantages, including surface modification capabilities, electron transfer properties, electron reservoir capacity, and a high surface area [26]. Because of their large number of active sites, these materials can easily form composites with CoFe-LDHs, improving their inherent catalytic performance [27].

In this work, we synthesized NDCDs, MoS2, and CoFe-LDH and further integrated these components into CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs as bifunctional electrocatalysts for water splitting applications. The synthesis process involved a simple hydrothermal carbonization method using fruit waste as the source of carbon. The resulting CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composites were thoroughly examined through various analytical methods, and their electrocatalytic performance for both the HER and the OER was evaluated using electrochemical techniques. The results show that CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs exhibited higher activity than previously reported CoFe-LDH composites [14] due to the incorporation of MoS2 and NDCDs. MoS2 has been extensively used in catalytic development owing to its abundant reaction sites and impressive electron transfer rate [28].

Through a simple hydrothermal method, NDCDs were successfully prepared from fruit waste. The synthesis process was visually confirmed by the noticeable change in color from green to brown. Subsequently, CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs were prepared from NDCDs, CoFe-LDH, and MoS2 by a hydrothermal method. The effective synthesis of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs was evident through a distinctive color transition from a green to a black precipitate.

Fig. 1 shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs, CoFe-LDH, and MoS2 composites. Peaks at 2θ values of 11.33°, 22.7°, 34.52°, 38.96°, 47.20°, 59.39° and 62.14° were assigned to the (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110), and (113) planes of CoFe-LDH (JCPDS 014–0191), respectively [27,28]. Additionally, the peaks at 14.58°, 33.67°, 39.38° and 58.19° agreed with the (002), (100), (103) and (110) planes of MoS2 [29]. Conversely, the XRD pattern of the NDCDs, as shown in Fig. S2, revealed a diffraction peak at 22.43°, indicative of a graphitic structure associated with the (002) plane. The broadening of this peak was ascribed to the small size of the NDCDs. Moreover, the presence of the (002) plane in the XRD pattern of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs provided clear evidence for the effective integration of MoS2 and NDCDs into the CoFe-LDH structure. Additional peaks of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs at 33.97°, 39.88°, and 59.19° were attributed to the (100), (103) and (110) planes of MoS2, confirming the incorporation of MoS2 into CoFe-LDH [30].

FE-SEM images of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs are shown in Fig. 2 a-b. Figs. S3c and S3d show the nanoflower-like morphology of pure MoS2. In contrast, Figs. S2a and S2b illustrate the microsphere-like structure of CoFe-LDH. Upon the incorporation of MoS2 and NDCDs into CoFe-LDH, the synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs exhibited a sandrose appearance, as depicted in Fig. 2a and b.

This sandrose-like structure comprising numerous intercrossed nanosheets, which facilitates electron transfer and electrolyte diffusion, enhancing the kinetics of the evolution process during electrolysis. Moreover, it offers a larger contact surface area with the electrolyte and increases the availability of active sites [31]. The structure of the layers in the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs was further elucidated through FE-TEM analysis (depicted in Fig. 2c–e). Remarkably, Fig. 2c and d clearly display the effective formation of layers, along with the discernible existence of NDCDs, MoS2, and CoFe-LDH. This was evidenced by the measured lattice fringes at 0.28 nm, 0.21 nm, and 0.39 nm for the NDCDs, MoS2, and CoFe-LDH, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 2e. These peaks matched well with the (002), (103), and (006) planes of the NDCDs, MoS2, and CoFe-LDH, respectively. Furthermore, the presence of Co, Fe, Mo, S, C, N, and O was confirmed through EDX spectroscopy and EDX mapping (Fig. S4).

The analysis of the nanostructures in the samples was extended using HR-TEM (Figs. S5a–d). The NDCDs size was calculated to be 2.2 nm from the HR-TEM image (Fig. S6). Notably, Fig. S4a reveals the coexistence of NDCDs, MoS2, and CoFe-LDH in the synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs. This figure confirmed the presence of NDCDs on the surfaces of the CoFe-LDH and MoS2 layers, facilitating electron transfer. Fig. S5b shows the interplanar spacings of CoFe-LDH and MoS2, indicating the successful growth of MoS2 between the layers of CoFe-LDH. Additional confirmation came from the lattice fringe values of CoFe-LDH and MoS2 in the synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs, which were 0.39 nm and 0.23 nm, respectively, as illustrated in Figs. S5c and S5d, and matched the (006) and (103) planes very well.

The chemical states of the synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Co 2p spectrum exhibited two distinct peaks (2p3/2 and 2p1/2) at 780.80 eV and 797.00 eV corresponding to Co3+, and the peaks at 786.60 eV and 802.40 eV were assigned to Co2+ (satellite) (Fig. 3 a) [32,33]. The peaks at 712.20 and 722.00 eV in Fig. 3b corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the presence of Fe3+ species [34]. Fig. 3c shows that the O 1s spectrum exhibited binding energy peaks at 531.40 eV and 532.40 eV, corresponding to the bands of oxygen in the M − O lattice and C–O/N–O, respectively [35]. In Fig. 3d, the Mo 3d spectrum exhibited a minor peak at 226.60 eV for S 2s and two characteristic peaks at 232.60 and 228.80 eV. These peaks correspond to the Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 binding energies of Mo4+, corroborating the formation of MoS2. Additionally, the peaks at 226.60 eV and 235.20 eV could be attributed to the Mo5+ and Mo6+ valence states, respectively. The presence of different valence states of Mo (+4 and + 6) suggested the coexistence of MoS2 and MoS3, with Mo5+ potentially arising from the partial reduction of Mo6+. The S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 spectrum revealed two peaks at 161.80 and 163.00 eV, respectively. Furthermore, a minor peak at 169.00 eV indicated the presence of bridging disulfides S4+, as shown in Fig. 3e [36].

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3f, the deconvolution spectrum of the N 1s level exhibited two peaks at 398.2 and 400.0 eV, corresponding to the secondary amine (N–H) and amide (CO–NH2) groups, respectively [37]. The C 1s binding energies at 284.8, 286.0, and 288.6 eV, as shown in Fig. S7, were attributed to C–N, C=C, and C=O functionalities, respectively.

Moreover, the surface properties of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs were analyzed by FTIR, and the resulting spectra were compared with those of CoFe-LDH, MoS2, and the NDCDs, as illustrated in Fig. S8. The band observed at 3256 cm−1 in the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs spectrum was attributed to –OH stretching. The absorption bands of MoS2 at 484, 904, 950, and 1626 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of Mo–S, S–S, Mo–O, and Mo=O, respectively, were evident in the FT-IR spectrum of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs. Similarly, the bands at 607, 934, and 1143 cm−1 representing Mo–S, Mo–O and S=O, respectively, were also present in the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs spectrum. Furthermore, the bands at 672, 800, and 1551 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of Co–O, Fe–O, and C=O, respectively. These findings confirmed the successful incorporation of CoFe-LDH, MoS2, and NDCDs into the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs nanocomposites [38–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of HER and OER
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a shows the LSV curves of various electrocatalysts used for the HER. The evaluated catalysts included bare Pt, CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF, CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF, CoFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF, CoFe-LDH/NF, MoS2/NF, bare NF, and NDCDs/NF. Whereas bare Pt demonstrated the most effective HER performance with a lower overpotential (−0.0098 VRHE), CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF, CoFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF, CoFe-LDH/NF, MoS2/NF, and NDCDs/NF necessitated higher overpotentials of −0.1955, −0.2290, −0.2470, 0.3180, and −0.4470 VRHE, respectively, to achieve −10 mA cm−2. The combination of CoFe-LDH with MoS2 and NDCDs in CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF led to higher HER performance; an exceptionally low overpotential of −0.1587 VRHE was needed to reach −10 mA cm−2 [43,44]. The synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF electrocatalytic HER activity was compared with recently reported CoFe LDH based materials and shown in Table S1.

The swiftest charge transfer rate was exhibited by CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF, as revealed in Fig. 5b by a lower Tafel slope of 77 mV⋅dec−1. This value surpassed those of CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF and CoFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF, which exhibited Tafel slopes of 89.3 and 97.8 mV⋅dec−1, respectively. The remarkable HER activity observed for CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF can be attributed to its advantageous Tafel-Volmer step, which initiates hydrogen evolution at a low overpotential in an alkaline medium. This process involves a sequence of reactions, including the Volmer (M + H2O + e → M-H* + OH), Heyrovsky (M-H* + H → M + H2*), and Tafel reactions (2M-H* → H2 + M + OH), ultimately resulting in the evolution of H2 [45,46].

Moreover, the OER activity of the samples was evaluated using the same approach as that for the HER, with RuO2 serving as the state-of-the-art catalyst. The LSV curves (as illustrated in Fig. 6 a) of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF demonstrated its superior OER activity; it required a lower overpotential (258.7 mV) than CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF (281.6 mV), CoFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF (359.9 mV), CoFe-LDH/NF (369.8 mV), and even RuO2/NF (328.5 mV) to achieve 10 mA cm−2 [47,48]. The synthesized CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF electrocatalytic OER activity were compared with recently reported CoFe LDH based materials and shown in Table S2.

The OER kinetics were evaluated by the Tafel slopes and EIS. CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF, CoFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF, and CoFe-LDH/NF exhibited Tafel slopes of 93, 123 and 139 mV⋅dec−1, respectively (as illustrated in Fig. 6b). However, the coupling of CoFe-LDH with MoS2 and NDCDs significantly reduced the Tafel slope of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF to 81 mV⋅dec-1, demonstrating accelerated OER kinetics. The notable electrocatalytic activity demonstrated by CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF can be ascribed not only to the improved OER kinetics but also to the structural arrangement of the material, which exposes more active sites. In essence, the material's outstanding performance could be ascribed to 2 key aspects: (1) the enhanced kinetics of the OER and (2) the increased availability of active sites for facilitating the reaction. Therefore, the electrocatalytic activity is evidently influenced by the distinctive combination of CoFe-LDH with MoS2 and NDCDs, which plays a pivotal role. This combination provides an extensive surface area for exposing active sites, resulting in a more efficient OER. The excellent OER activity under alkaline conditions could be described by the following reactions [49,50].
(a)
* + OH −
→
HO * +
e −
(b)
HO * + OH
− →
O * +
e −
+
H 2
O
(c)
O * + O *
→
O 2
+ 2 *
(d)
O * + OH
− →
HOO * +
e −
(e)
HOO * + OH
− →
O 2
+
e −
+
H 2
O
Overall
oxygen
evolution
reaction : 4
OH −
→
O 2
+ 4
e −
+ 2
H 2
O

Here, * signifies the electrocatalyst's active site (Co/Fe/Mo), and OH-, O*, and HOO* indicate adsorbed intermediate species. The initial step involves the adsorption of an HO* ion onto an active site, followed by its reaction with a second HO* ion. The resultant O* then undergoes a reaction with HO* to yield the HOO* intermediate, culminating in the generation of O2.

To delve deeper into the kinetics and conduct a more thorough analysis, EIS was utilized to gain insight into the reaction kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The Nyquist plot of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF (Fig. 9) revealed a charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 68 Ω/cm2, which was considerably lower than those of CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF (87 Ω/cm2) and bare NF (120 Ω/cm2), as indicated by the corresponding equivalent circuits (Fig. S10). Notably, the conductivity increased, and Rct decreased for CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF due to the electron-carrying capabilities of MoS2 and the NDCDs positioned within the layers of CoFe-LDH [51].

To thoroughly assess the effective catalytic performance of the developed electrocatalysts, the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the electrode was determined. This was accomplished by measuring the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) at various scan rates via cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Fig. 8
and Fig. S11). The values revealed that the Cdl of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF was the highest (0.00184 mF/cm2), surpassing those of CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF (0.0017 mF/cm2) and CoFe-LDH/NF (0.00134 mF/cm2). An elevated Cdl signifies an improved ECSA and simultaneously enhances the performance of the electrocatalyst. Consequently, the ECSA of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF was calculated to be 0.046 cm2, whereas for CoFe-LDH@MoS2/NF and CoFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF, the ECSA values were 0.0425 cm2 and 0.0335 cm2, respectively [52].

Additionally, the redox-active surface areas of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs, CoFe-LDH@MoS2, and CoFe-LDH catalysts were determined by assessing the reduction peak by excluding the oxidation peak from the redox couple. The results are presented in Fig. 9. The calculated surface area values for CoFe-LDH, CoFe-LDH@MoS2 and the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs are 0.000200, 0.000301 and 0.000498 VA, respectively. These findings suggest a greater number of exposed redox active sites on the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs, indicating its superiority in the OER and HER. Furthermore, a TOF calculation was conducted using the redox area curve at a fixed potential of 1.53 V. The resulting values are illustrated in a bar diagram (Fig. 10
). The ESI provides a comprehensive explanation of the procedure for calculating the TOF values.

The TOF results highlight that the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs (2.90/s for the HER and 1.54/s for the OER) exhibited superior activity for the HER and OER compared to the other electrocatalysts. TOF of the synthesized electrocatalyst were compared with recently published CoFe LDH related works and shown in Table S3. To scrutinize the intrinsic activities of the prepared catalysts, ECSA normalization was performed, and the corresponding ECSA-normalized LSV is illustrated in Fig. S12. The activity trend observed in the ECSA-normalized LSV curve reflects that of the geometrically normalized polarized LSV curve. The evaluation of CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF extended beyond assessing its electrocatalytic activity; its durability and stability were also scrutinized for 24 h (Fig. 11 ). Remarkably, the observed potential change was very minimal. These findings indicate that CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF exhibited good performance in terms of stability and longevity [53,54].
The outstanding performance of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs/NF composite in both the HER and OER can be attributed to the following factors:

1. Compared with the structures of CoFe-LDH and MoS2 alone, the sandrose structure of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs exhibited a significantly larger electrochemically active area and greater number of accessible catalytic sites (refer to Fig. 2). These structural characteristics led to enhanced performance. Additionally, the BET results indicate an increase in the surface area from 11.3 m2/g (CoFe-LDH@MoS2) to 12.4 m2/g (CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs) after the modification of CoFe-LDH/MoS2 with NDCDs.

2. The redox active surface area of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite surpasses those of the other two composites, as illustrated in Fig. 9. The incorporation of NDCDs into CoFe-LDH@MoS2 improved the pathways for rapid charge transfer, as depicted in Fig. 7. Furthermore, this integration provides protection against corrosion or agglomeration in alkaline media, resulting in the high stability of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs.

In this investigation, we applied a simple hydrothermal approach to synthesize NDCDs from fruit waste. Subsequently, a CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite was synthesized from NDCDs, CoFe-LDH, and MoS2. The sandrose structure of the synthesized composite was characterized by FE-SEM, HR-TEM, EDX, XPS, XRD, and FT-IR. The electrocatalytic performances of the NDCDs, CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs, CoFe-LDH, and CoFe-LDH@MoS2 composites in the HER and OER were meticulously assessed using methods such as LSV, Tafel analysis, chronopotentiometry, CV, and EIS in a 1 M KOH solution. Remarkably, the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite demonstrated exceptional catalytic activity for both the HER and OER. The intrinsic activities, such as the TOF and specific activity, of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite were calculated. Furthermore, the synthesized composite exhibited greater stability over 24 h, as validated by chronopotentiometry. This study substantiates the effectiveness of the CoFe-LDH/MoS2@NDCDs composite, positioning it as an efficient non-noble metal electrocatalyst and paving the way for large-scale water electrolysis in the future.

Chandrasekaran Pitchai and Chih-Ming Chen thank the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan for funding fellowship (NSTC 112-2811-E−005-002 and NSTC 110-2221-E−005-006-MY3). Chih-Ming Chen thank the financial support from the “Innovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture” from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Chandrasekaran Pitchai: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mahalakshmi Vedanarayanan: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chih-Ming Chen: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Sethuraman Mathur Gopalakrishnan: Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
